explanation: Rosa rugosa flower extract provides polyphenols, flavonoids, and gallotannins with antioxidant, astringent, and skin-conditioning properties. It is well-tolerated at cosmetic extract concentrations and used in protective and toning formulas for its antioxidant content; individuals with fragrance sensitivity should be aware that rose extracts may contain trace regulated allergens.

Rosmarinus officinalis (rosemary) leaf oil is a rosemary essential oil rich in camphor, borneol, eucalyptol, and linalool — multiple documented contact sensitizers and EU-regulated fragrance allergens. It is flagged for its sensitization potential and can trigger allergic contact dermatitis and an itch-and-scratch cycle on reactive or fragrance-sensitive skin, particularly in leave-on products.

Rosmarinus officinalis leaf extract provides rosmarinic acid, carnosic acid, and carnosol — some of the most potent naturally occurring antioxidants — with exceptional free-radical-scavenging, anti-inflammatory, and UV-protective properties. It is well-tolerated at cosmetic extract concentrations and used in anti-aging and protective formulas for its extraordinary antioxidant density; it is distinct from rosemary essential oil and does not share its sensitization risk.

Rosmarinus officinalis leaf oil is rosemary essential oil rich in camphor, borneol, eucalyptol, and linalool — the same ingredient as Rosmarinus Officinalis (Rosemary) Leaf Oil, listed without the common name parenthetical. It is flagged for its sensitization potential and can trigger allergic contact dermatitis on reactive or fragrance-sensitive skin, particularly in leave-on products.

Royal jelly extract is derived from the bee-secreted substance rich in 10-hydroxy-2-decenoic acid (10-HDA), proteins, amino acids, and B vitamins with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and skin-conditioning properties. It is generally well-tolerated at cosmetic extract concentrations — though bee-product allergy is a contraindication — and used in nourishing and anti-aging formulas for its bioactive fatty acid and protein content.

Rubus idaeus (raspberry) fruit extract provides anthocyanins, ellagic acid, and quercetin antioxidants with potent free-radical-scavenging, anti-inflammatory, and brightening properties. It is well-tolerated and used in protective and brightening formulas for its high antioxidant content.

Saccharomyces ferment is a yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) fermentation product providing a complex of amino acids, polysaccharides, B vitamins, and bioactive metabolites with skin-conditioning and barrier-supporting properties. It is well-tolerated and used in ferment-forward and nourishing formulas for its biomimetic, bioavailable nutrient content.

Saccharomyces ferment filtrate is the filtered liquid fraction of Saccharomyces cerevisiae (yeast) fermentation providing concentrated amino acids, B vitamins, and bioactive metabolites with skin-conditioning, brightening, and barrier-supporting properties. It is well-tolerated and used in ferment-forward and brightening formulas for its bioavailable bioactive content.

Saccharum officinarum (sugarcane) extract provides glycolic acid — the smallest and most skin-penetrating AHA — along with sucrose, polyphenols, and flavonoids with mild exfoliant, brightening, and antioxidant properties. It is generally well-tolerated at cosmetic extract concentrations and used in brightening and mild resurfacing formulas for its natural AHA and antioxidant content.

Sage (Salvia officinalis) extract provides rosmarinic acid, ursolic acid, and flavonoid antioxidants with potent free-radical-scavenging, antimicrobial, and astringent properties. It is generally well-tolerated at cosmetic extract concentrations and used in protective and purifying formulas for its antioxidant and antimicrobial content; it is distinct from sage essential oil and does not share its sensitization risk.

Sage extract (Salvia officinalis) provides rosmarinic acid, flavonoids, and terpene antioxidants with antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties — the same ingredient as Sage (Salvia Officinalis) Extract, listed without the botanical name. It is generally well-tolerated at cosmetic extract concentrations and used in protective and purifying formulas; it is distinct from sage essential oil and does not share its sensitization risk.

This entry is a data artifact — a misspelling of 'salicylic acid' that refers to the same ingredient: salicylic acid, a beta hydroxy acid exfoliant and antimicrobial active used to treat acne and clear pores. The correct INCI name is Salicylic Acid.

Salicylic acid is a beta hydroxy acid (BHA) exfoliant that is oil-soluble and uniquely able to penetrate into pores to dissolve excess sebum, comedones, and desquamate the pore lining — the gold standard OTC treatment for acne-prone and congestion-prone skin. It is flagged as a BHA exfoliant; at effective concentrations (0.5–2%) it can be irritating and barrier-disrupting on sensitive or compromised skin, and benefits from gradual introduction alongside daily sun protection.

Salix alba (white willow) bark extract is naturally rich in salicin and related salicylates — the natural precursor compounds to aspirin and salicylic acid — with anti-inflammatory and mild exfoliant properties. It is generally well-tolerated at cosmetic extract concentrations; individuals with aspirin or salicylate sensitivity should approach with caution.

Salvia officinalis leaf extract is the INCI form of sage leaf extract — providing rosmarinic acid, carnosol, and flavonoid antioxidants with potent free-radical-scavenging, antimicrobial, and anti-inflammatory properties. It is generally well-tolerated at cosmetic extract concentrations and used in protective and purifying formulas; it is distinct from sage essential oil (Salvia Officinalis Oil) and does not share its sensitization risk.

Salvia officinalis oil is sage essential oil rich in camphor, thujone, and 1,8-cineole with documented contact sensitization risk due to its terpenoid content. It is flagged for its sensitization potential and can trigger allergic contact dermatitis and an itch-and-scratch cycle on reactive or fragrance-sensitive skin, particularly in leave-on products.

Saponaria officinalis (soapwort) leaf extract provides saponins — natural surfactant glycosides — along with flavonoids with gentle cleansing, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties. It is generally well-tolerated at cosmetic extract concentrations and used in gentle cleansing and protective formulas for its mild natural saponin surfactant and antioxidant content.

Sapphire powder is finely milled corundum (aluminum oxide, Al₂O₃) used in cosmetics as a luxury optical ingredient that provides soft-focus light diffusion and a smooth, velvety skin texture. It is considered inert, non-sensitizing, and well-tolerated, functioning as a texture and optical modifier rather than a skin-active ingredient.

Scutellaria baicalensis root extract (Chinese skullcap) provides baicalin, baicalein, and wogonin — flavonoids with some of the most thoroughly studied anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and anti-androgenic properties in dermatology — with documented efficacy in reducing rosacea erythema, acne inflammation, and free-radical damage. It is exceptionally well-tolerated and used in calming, blemish-targeting, and protective formulas for its exceptional anti-inflammatory flavonoid density.

SD Alcohol 40 (specially denatured ethanol) is denatured alcohol used in skincare as a solvent, antimicrobial, and astringent. It is flagged as a drying solvent — at higher concentrations it dissolves the skin's intercellular lipids and causes barrier disruption, increased transepidermal water loss, and can trigger an itch-and-scratch cycle, making it a poor choice for dry, reactive, or barrier-compromised skin.

Sebacic acid is a naturally occurring 10-carbon dicarboxylic acid found in sebum, used in skincare for its mild antimicrobial activity against Cutibacterium acnes and Malassezia, and as a dicarboxylic acid related to azelaic acid with similar mild brightening and antimicrobial properties. It is well-tolerated and used in blemish-targeting and barrier-supportive formulas.

Serica soie refers to silk protein — "serica" (Latin) and "soie" (French) both mean silk — derived from Bombyx mori silk cocoons and used as a film-forming, conditioning, and humectant agent that deposits on skin and hair for a smooth, luminous feel. It is well-tolerated and non-sensitizing at typical cosmetic concentrations.

The trailing parenthesis in "Serica Soie)" is a data artifact from ingredient label parsing; this is the same ingredient as Serica Soie — silk protein derived from Bombyx mori cocoons used as a film-forming and conditioning agent for skin and hair. It is well-tolerated and non-sensitizing at typical cosmetic concentrations.

Sesamum indicum (sesame) seed extract provides sesamin, sesamolin, and polyphenol antioxidants with free-radical-scavenging and skin-conditioning properties. It is generally well-tolerated at cosmetic extract concentrations; individuals with sesame food allergy should confirm skin-contact cross-reactivity before use, as sesame is a common contact allergen in allergic individuals.

SH-polypeptide-121 is a synthetic signal polypeptide used in anti-aging formulas to stimulate collagen and extracellular matrix protein synthesis for improved skin firmness and resilience. It is well-tolerated, non-irritating, and used in firming serums and creams for its matrix-stimulating properties.

Shea butter (Butyrospermum parkii butter) is a rich plant fat with a unique fatty acid profile of oleic acid (~40%), stearic acid (~40%), and the triterpene lupeol with anti-inflammatory properties, providing deep emollient moisturization and excellent barrier repair. It is exceptionally well-tolerated, non-comedogenic at typical cosmetic concentrations, and widely used in moisturizers and protective formulas for its superior emollient and barrier-repairing properties.

Shea butter cetyl esters are a modified shea butter fraction esterified with cetyl alcohol, providing a lighter, more elegant skin feel than raw shea butter while retaining its nourishing fatty acid profile. They are well-tolerated, non-comedogenic, and used in emollient formulas for a more elegant shea-derived conditioning effect.

Shea butter ethyl esters are shea butter fatty acids esterified with ethanol, producing a lightweight, fast-absorbing shea-derived emollient with the nourishing fatty acid profile of shea butter in a less occlusive, more elegant form. They are well-tolerated and used in lighter moisturizers and hair care for their comfortable texture and shea-derived conditioning.

Shea butter glycereth-8 esters are shea butter fatty acids esterified with glycereth-8 (a glycerol ethoxylate), producing a water-dispersible, lightly conditioning shea-derived emollient with both skin-feel and mild humectant properties. They are well-tolerated and used in moisturizing formulas for their skin-compatible shea fatty acid content in a more water-compatible form.

Shea butter glyceride is a glyceryl ester of shea butter fatty acids used as a mild emollient and co-emulsifying agent with the skin-compatible fatty acid profile of shea butter in a more easily incorporated form. It is well-tolerated and non-comedogenic, used in moisturizing and protective formulas for its nourishing, barrier-supportive properties.
